Lyngby Half Marathon · April 26 2026 · 21.1 km

Testing
Running
Myths

We are a group of friends who decided to stop arguing about running advice and actually test it. 25 of us lined up at the Lyngby Half Marathon, GPS watches on, training logs ready, and let the data do the talking.

25Runners

Meet the Group

Three Kinds of Runners

25 friends who trained differently, ran the same course, had very different days. Click any name to see their profile.

The Experienced
5+ half marathons, structured training, sub-90 target. Race-tested strategy and proven pacing instincts built over years.
The Grinders
40+ km/week, 10+ training weeks, volume-first approach. Endurance built through sheer repetition. May not always have the raw speed, but they never quit.
The Believers
Fewer races, lower weekly volume, fuelled by ambition. Running on heart. The data often underestimates them, sometimes correctly, sometimes not.
Data Model

Predicted vs Actual

We built a regression model on training load, blended with each runner's own stated target time. How well did the data know them?

Predicted vs Actual Finish Time: All Runners

Bars = actual finish time, coloured by archetype. Triangles = model prediction (blend of OLS regression on training load and each runner's own stated target time).

The Route

The Course

21.1 km through Lyngby: a rolling northern loop, a vicious climb at km 14, then the long run home. Select a runner to see their pace heatmap.

Fast
Slow
· Hover elevation chart to sync cursor on map

Full Elevation Profile (6,759 GPS Trackpoints)

0–4 km
Opening Push
Fresh legs, crowded roads. Experienced runners settle in; Believers fight the urge to sprint.
4–13 km
North & West Loops
Rolling terrain through the suburbs. Heart rates settle, rhythms find themselves. The halfway split tells the story early.
13–16 km
Killer Climb
3 km of sustained ascent. Hill-trained runners hold form; the rest pay the price in heart rate and pace.
16–21 km
The Final Push
Post-climb survival. Who recovered quickest? Experience shows here — the Grinders fight to hold pace while the Experienced open their stride to the finish.
01
Supported

Experienced runners
pace themselves better

The claim: the biggest trap in a half marathon is starting too fast. Race-day excitement pushes you to go out harder than planned — the first 5 km feels effortless. But running above your target pace burns through energy reserves early, and by km 15–17 your legs give out and you "blow up": pace drops sharply as you struggle to the finish. Experienced runners know this trap and deliberately hold back early, saving energy for a strong second half. We measured pacing discipline using a split ratio: your average pace per km in the second half ÷ your first half. A ratio of 1.0 = perfectly even. Above 1.0 = you slowed down (burned out). Below 1.0 = you ran the second half faster — a negative split, the gold standard of race execution. The prediction: experienced runners cluster near or below 1.0; less experienced runners skew above it.

Prior: Haney & Mercer (2011) found that recreational runners with more experience show significantly smaller positive splits; experience directly improves in-race pacing judgement.

Split Ratio by Archetype (individual runners)

Each dot = one runner. Values > 1.0 = slowed down in 2nd half. Yellow line = even split.

Km-by-km Pace (select to compare)

Finding: The cluster averages (◆ markers on the chart) tell the story clearly. Experienced runners averaged 0.978 — they ran the second half faster, a disciplined negative split. Grinders averaged 0.981 — almost perfectly even, the textbook controlled effort. Believers averaged 1.035 — the classic blow-up pattern: go out ambitious, fade hard. The three groups land in exactly the predicted order. Our data supports this myth clearly: experience and discipline translate directly into smarter pacing.

02
Supported

Hill training helps
on a hilly course

The claim: runners who specifically trained on hills before the race will absorb the Lyngby km 13–16 climb better than those who only did flat training. We measured this by comparing heart-rate spikes and pace degradation on the climb for hill-trained vs non-hill-trained runners.

Prior: Billat et al. (2003) showed hill-specific training improves VO2max utilisation on inclines and reduces the cardiac cost of uphill running.

HR + Pace Profile at the Climb (groups compared)

Lines = avg pace per km. Shaded = killer climb (km 13–16). Red = heart rate (right axis).

Pace Drop at the Climb (per runner)

Pace increase (slowdown) during km 13–16 vs km 1–12 baseline. Sorted by impact. Cyan = hill-trained.

Finding: Hill-trained runners show a smaller HR spike at the climb (+8–12 bpm vs +15–22 bpm) and hold better pace through km 13–16. Non-trained runners' pace degradation is noticeably steeper, and many never fully recovered in the final 5 km.

03
Partially Supported

More training always
means faster times

The claim: simply running more km per week in the build-up to the race leads to better finish times. We measured the Pearson correlation between self-reported weekly km and actual finish time, and looked at whether the relationship holds across archetypes.

Prior: Barandun et al. (2012) found that weekly training volume is a significant predictor of half-marathon race time in recreational runners, but explains only part of the variance — training quality and running economy account for much of the rest.

Training Volume vs Finish Time (click legend to filter)

Finding: A moderate negative correlation exists (r ≈ −0.55); more volume tends to mean faster times. But the relationship is non-linear and within-group variance is large. The Experienced runners finish faster even at lower volume, suggesting quality and experience matter as much as quantity.

04
Partially Supported

Interval training leads
to smarter race strategy

The claim: interval training teaches your body to manage effort under fatigue, leading not just to faster times but to smarter race execution — specifically more even or negative splits. We compared finish times and split strategies between runners who reported doing intervals and those who only ran easy.

Prior: Helgerud et al. (2007) showed that high-intensity interval training significantly improves VO2max and running economy, allowing athletes to sustain a higher percentage of maximal pace for longer.

Finish Time Distribution: interval vs no-interval trainers

Split Strategy (who ran smarter?)

Negative = sped up · Even = <3% diff · Positive = slowed down

Finding: Interval trainers finish on average 7 minutes faster and show a higher proportion of even and negative splits, suggesting better race-day pacing awareness, not just raw speed. Worth noting: in our group, interval training also correlates with higher weekly volume and more race experience, so the effect is likely a combination of all three.

Our Findings

What the data says

After 25 GPS tracks, 4 myths, and a lot of km. Here is what we actually found.

01
Experienced runners pace better
Supported
The Experienced group shows split ratios tightly clustered around 1.0. Experience is the strongest predictor of even pacing, stronger than training volume or fitness metrics alone.
02
Hill training helps on hilly courses
Supported
On a hilly course like the Lyngby half marathon, hill training proved to be effective. Hill-trained runners absorbed the km 13-16 climb with smaller HR spikes and held better pace through to the finish.
03
More training = faster times
Partially Supported
There is a real correlation (r ≈ −0.55), but the Experienced group often outperforms higher-volume Believers. Quality, experience, and specificity matter as much as raw mileage.
04
Intervals lead to smarter strategy
Partially Supported
Interval trainers averaged 7 minutes faster and ran more even splits. The mechanism seems to be improved pace judgement under fatigue, not just aerobic capacity.
How Do We Compare?

vs Copenhagen HM

How did our group perform relative to a typical half marathon field? We compare against the Copenhagen Half Marathon — one of Scandinavia's largest races, with thousands of finishers across all ages and levels.

Finish Time Distribution: Our Group vs Copenhagen HM Field

Copenhagen HM representative sample (n=500). Our group n=25. Scaled for comparison. Note: our group is 23-26 years old (prime running age), while the Copenhagen field is open to all ages, with many participants in their 30s and 40s.

Context: Our group median (≈ 109 min) is about 6 minutes faster than the Copenhagen median (115 min). This gap is expected: we are a self-selected, motivated cohort aged 23–27, all of whom tracked training for weeks leading up to race day. The Copenhagen field is open to all ages and abilities, with a large contingent of casual and first-time runners in their 30s and 40s.

Top performers: Célien, Pablo Bauri and Oriol would rank in roughly the top 6–8% of a Copenhagen field — competitive amateur territory. At the other end, Coline and Carlos finish right in the heart of the typical recreational field, ahead of roughly 35–45% of participants.

Spread: Our IQR is roughly 20 min vs Copenhagen's ~33 min. This is a self-selection effect — we all trained together, shared advice, and ran the same course. We converged more than a random urban field would.

Copenhagen HM reference: approximate distribution from publicly reported aggregate results (~2019 edition). Sample n ≈ 500 used for histogram comparison. Percentile estimates use a normal approximation (μ = 115.4, σ = 23.8 min).

Social Data Analysis · DTU 2026 · Marta Arana · Esben Kok · Sergi Lupon
References
  • 1 Smits, B.L.M. et al. (2019). The relationship between psychological factors and performance in recreational runners. Frontiers in Psychology, 10, 3026.
  • 2 Vikmoen, O. et al. (2013). The effects of uphill vs. level grade treadmill training on VO₂max and performance. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research, 27(6).
  • 3 Barandun, U. et al. (2012). Predictors of half-marathon race time in male recreational runners. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research.
  • 4 Helgerud, J. et al. (2007). Aerobic high-intensity intervals improve VO₂max more than moderate training. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise, 39(4), 665–671. PubMed
  • Data: GPS tracks from 25 runners, Lyngby Halvmarathon 26 April 2026. Survey self-reported pre-race via Google Forms. Four synthetic entries added post-race from GPS-only runners.
